title: Beale Street, Black Music & Cultural Memory
summary: Start on Beale, then move through blues authorship, Black church and press history, civic enterprise, and the museums that keep Memphis music memory alive.

stops.title: Beale Street
stops.description: Open on Beale Street itself, where Black Memphis performance, nightlife, commerce, and public memory still shape how the city hears itself.

stops.title: W.C. Handy Home & Museum
stops.description: Move into blues authorship and songwriting memory at the home and museum tied to one of Beale's defining musical figures.

stops.title: Beale Street Baptist Church
stops.description: Add Black church life and Ida B. Wells context here, so the route reads Beale as a place of resistance, organizing, and institution-building as well as music.

stops.title: Robert Church Park
stops.description: Turn toward Black enterprise and civic memory at a park named for the businessman and community builder who helped shape Beale's public legacy.

stops.title: Memphis Rock 'n' Soul Museum
stops.description: Broaden the route from one street into the larger Memphis music story, where labor, migration, and performance all feed the city's sound.

stops.title: Blues Hall of Fame
stops.description: Close at the Blues Hall of Fame, where Beale's local sound turns into canon, recognition, and long-view cultural memory.

title: Civil Rights, MLK & Public Memory
summary: Follow Memphis through organizing ground, the sanitation workers' strike, Dr. King's final speech, the Lorraine Motel, and the longer Black resistance traditions that frame them.

stops.title: Clayborn Temple
stops.description: Open at Clayborn Temple, the strike-era gathering ground where Black Memphis organizing became impossible to miss in 1968.

stops.title: I AM A MAN Plaza
stops.description: Move into the public memory of the sanitation workers' strike at a plaza that makes Black labor dignity and civic witness central to the route.

stops.title: Mason Temple
stops.description: Stand at Mason Temple, where Dr. King delivered the Mountaintop speech and where movement urgency, spiritual power, and Memphis history meet.

stops.title: National Civil Rights Museum
stops.description: Turn to the Lorraine Motel and the National Civil Rights Museum, where local struggle is held inside the longer national story of Black freedom work.

stops.title: Beale Street Baptist Church
stops.description: Close by widening the route into Black church, press, and resistance traditions that stretch beyond 1968 and help explain why Memphis mattered.

title: Stax, South Memphis & Creative Power
summary: Move through South Memphis by way of Stax, neighborhood music memory, Black radio, and the creative institutions that made soul sound local and global at once.

stops.title: Stax Museum of American Soul Music
stops.description: Open at Stax, where South Memphis studio craft, Black ownership, and neighborhood sound made soul music a world language.

stops.title: The Soulsville Foundation
stops.description: Stay in the Soulsville campus to read creative power through education, preservation, and the effort to keep South Memphis cultural memory rooted in place.

stops.title: WDIA Radio Legacy Site
stops.description: Turn toward Black radio history and public voice at a stop that lets the route connect soul music to the stations that carried Black Memphis into everyday life.

stops.title: Overton Park Shell
stops.description: Add a civic performance stage where Black Memphis music, public gathering, and wider city audiences met in the open air.

stops.title: Hattiloo Theatre
stops.description: Close with Black performance and storytelling still in motion at a theater that keeps Memphis creative power contemporary, local, and self-directed.

title: Orange Mound & Black Neighborhood Legacy
summary: Read Orange Mound through homeownership, neighborhood institutions, Black education, and the everyday places that make community continuity visible in Memphis.

stops.title: Melrose High School
stops.description: Open at Melrose, where Black Memphis education, neighborhood pride, and generations of local leadership give Orange Mound one of its clearest institutional anchors.

stops.title: Orange Mound Community Center
stops.description: Move into everyday neighborhood infrastructure at a community center that helps the route read Orange Mound through recreation, gathering, and local continuity.

stops.title: Mt. Moriah Missionary Baptist Church
stops.description: Add faith and neighborhood memory at a Black church stop that lets the route read Orange Mound through spiritual life, leadership, and community care.

stops.title: Dunbar Elementary Legacy Site
stops.description: Turn to block-level educational memory at a school legacy stop that helps explain how homeownership and neighborhood identity were carried forward in everyday life.

stops.title: Orange Mound Historic Corridor
stops.description: Close in the neighborhood itself, reading streets, houses, and local business memory as evidence of one of the country's earliest Black homeownership communities.

title: River, Labor & Black Memphis
summary: Read Black Memphis through the riverfront, labor history, sanitation-worker memory, civic industry, and the movement of people and goods that shaped the city.

stops.title: Mississippi River Park
stops.description: Open on the riverfront, where Black Memphis can be read through movement, trade, labor, and the geography that made the city a corridor as much as a destination.

stops.title: Slave Haven Underground Railroad Museum
stops.description: Add freedom routes and Black mobility through a site that helps the route connect river geography to escape, resistance, and early Memphis Black history.

stops.title: Clayborn Temple
stops.description: Turn back to labor organizing at Clayborn Temple, where the sanitation strike gives Black Memphis working history a sharp local center.

stops.title: I AM A MAN Plaza
stops.description: Stay with labor dignity and public witness at a plaza that makes Black work, protest, and civic memory inseparable in Memphis.

stops.title: Memphis Rock 'n' Soul Museum
stops.description: Close where labor, migration, and performance are read together, making clear that Black Memphis work shaped the city's culture as much as its economy.
